text: Hello and welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're going to focus on ETEP retrorectus and how to manage posterior defects. The case is a seventy-five-year-old man with a history of colonic perforation who had a lap converted to open colectomy. He presented with a medical history notable for COPD and diabetes, though these were optimized prior to the surgery. Let's jump into the cross-sectional imaging. You'll note a very wide rectus on both sides, favorable anatomy for ETEP. I think they measured around nine to ten centimeters, and then multifocal defects and diastasis in the midline. One of the other things I noted on this cross-sectional imaging was that the patient had a relative lack in preperitoneal fat, especially around the area of the hernias.

And so I definitely was not too enthusiastic about a transabdominal approach or a preperitoneal approach. Hernia dimensions were six by eleven, thin peritoneum with multifocal defects. So in terms of the case setup, planned for a left retromuscular lateral doc with ETEP approach. And then we're sort of mentally prepared for a thin peritoneum. and a high likelihood of having peritoneal fenestrations or holes. So ETAP initial access, I use an optical port, I use the applied medical first entry trocar, which is nice because you can insufflate while the inner cannula is still inside. more retromuscular here. Initial insufflation was done with low flow, which isn't my preference, and so it took a while to establish that view that I usually get.

The surgeon enters the areolar plane and places trocars on the lateral aspect of the rectus space. Using laparoscopic instruments, the initial dissection is performed with careful energy application to minimize bleeding. The robot is docked, and standard tools such as force bipolar and monopolar scissors are used. Subtle differences in the fascia are visible around the hernia area.

The surgeon incises the posterior sheath, leaving a one-centimeter cuff, and begins pre-peritoneal dissection. Visual cues indicate scarring and a thin peritoneum near the hernia sac, prompting the surgeon to work more cephalad to develop the space. The contralateral rectus is accessed, and the retromuscular space is developed by teasing the peritoneum away from the sheath to avoid entering the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon is developing a dissection and notices a hole in the thin peritoneum. They focus on recruiting as much of the hernia sac as possible, pulling it down into the field to aid in eventual posterior closure. The thinness of the sac is evident, and the surgeon avoids transecting it prematurely.

The surgeon performs a retrorectus dissection, unifying the inferior and superior dissection for the contralateral rectus. Multifocal defects, diastasis, and a prominent hernia are visible, along with an umbilical hernia. The left-sided dissection appears solid, while the right side requires further work in the lower abdomen. A large posterior defect is addressed with retrorectus dissection, and the surgeon considers using autologous tissue like omentum or falciform, although challenging in a robotic setting.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the tissue, focusing on a wide dissection around the retroactive space. The posterior area is visibly tented up, indicating tension that needs to be addressed. The dissection extends well beyond the hernia margins to offload tension from the posterior.

The surgeon works on the inferior aspect of the contralateral rectus, progressing towards the epigastric region. The retromuscular space is unified from the left and right sides, achieving a satisfying dissection with minimal bleeding. The right rectus is mobilized, and the flap lays flat on the viscera. The dissection continues below the arcuate line, resulting in a loose flap that can be closed without tension. The insufflation pressure is maintained between twelve and fifteen to visually assess tension during closure.

The surgeon completes the posterior closure and begins the anterior fascial closure, starting below the hernia defect. Using a number one Stratafix suture on a CT-one needle, the surgeon sequentially tightens the fascia, addressing the lower diastasis. The sutures are run back on themselves to ensure complete muscle re-approximation, with three 18-inch Stratafix sutures used in total.

The surgeon places a 22 by 26 centimeter Bartzoff's macroporous polypropylene mesh into the wide retro-rectus space. Basic tissue fixation is performed to the posterior, using a sprayer for application. The mesh is positioned to offload tension on the posterior tissue.
